one_line_takeaway: CleanBase detects RAG prompt-injection documents by finding cliques of anomalously similar embeddings in the knowledge database (avg. FPR 1.9%, cutting HotpotQA PoisonedRAG ASR from 97% to 6%), but a white-box adversary that explicitly minimizes cross-document similarity, or simply keeps fewer than three malicious documents per target question, evades it almost entirely (FNR up to 96-100%).
key_finding: Across six datasets and nine attacks, CleanBase achieves an average FPR of 1.9% (vs. 27.9% for PromptGuard and 0.4% for DataSentinel) while its average FNR ranges from 0.0% up to 34.2% depending on attack type, substantially better than DataSentinel (19.0%-99.2% average FNR range) and PromptGuard (0.0%-44.8%). CleanBase is near-perfect (FNR 0.0%) against PoisonedRAG-B and GASLITEing but weaker against PoisonedRAG-W (34.2% average FNR) and Phantom (16.5% average FNR), because those attacks don't explicitly enforce high similarity among a target question's malicious documents. End-to-end, CleanBase reduces average ASR across all nine attacks/six datasets to 9%, and for HotpotQA under PoisonedRAG specifically the paper reports ASR dropping from 97% to 6%. Ablations show both Step I (kNN graph) and Step II (statistical pruning) are necessary -- removing Step II alone pushes FPR above 99%. Cosine similarity and the Contriever-msmarco embedding model give the best FPR/FNR trade-off versus dot product/Euclidean distance and ANCE/Contriever alternatives. Under adaptive white-box attacks that explicitly minimize pairwise malicious-document similarity while preserving retrieval relevance (built on PoisonedRAG-W), CleanBase's average FNR rises to 96.1% and post-defense ASR rises to 37.3%, and capping malicious documents per target question at <=2 defeats CleanBase entirely by construction (a clique requires >=3 nodes) -- but the paper shows these evasion strategies substantially reduce the attacker's own baseline (no-defense) ASR (e.g., PoisonedRAG-W ASR on NQ drops from 90% to 60% just from reducing inter-document similarity, before CleanBase is even applied).
core_contribution: CleanBase, a database-side defense for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems that detects and removes malicious (prompt-injected) documents from the knowledge database before deployment, rather than hardening the LLM or retriever at query time. It builds a k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) similarity graph over document embeddings (Step I), prunes edges below a statistically derived threshold tau = mu + z*sigma computed from a random sample of edge weights (Step II), and flags documents that form cliques (subgraphs of >=3 mutually connected nodes, detected via Bron-Kerbosch) in the pruned graph as malicious (Step III). The paper also derives theoretical upper bounds on CleanBase's false positive rate (FPR) and false negative rate (FNR) under a formal similarity-distribution model (Theorems 1 and 2, proofs in Appendices C/D).
threat_model: Standard RAG prompt-injection threat model following Zou et al. 2025 (PoisonedRAG). Attacker goal: manipulate a RAG system so that user questions from an attacker-chosen target set produce attacker-specified target answers, while leaving non-target-question behavior unaffected (stealth). Attacker capability: insert multiple malicious documents (each containing a crafted injected prompt) into the knowledge database, realistic when the database ingests untrusted web content (e.g., Wikipedia-sourced corpora); attacker has no access to benign documents or the LLM, and either black-box or white-box access to the retriever. Defender: has access only to the knowledge database (not necessarily the retriever or LLM used downstream), may use a different embedding model f' than the RAG system's own embedding model f, and removes flagged documents before the sanitized database is used to serve RAG queries. This is a first-line, deploy-before-use defense, explicitly contrasted with runtime defenses that secure the LLM (fine-tuning) or the retriever (filter-at-query-time).
limitations: The paper's own findings show CleanBase's core assumption -- that malicious documents targeting the same question are highly mutually similar -- is also its central vulnerability: attacks that don't optimize for cross-document similarity (PoisonedRAG-W, Phantom) or that explicitly minimize it under white-box knowledge of the detector evade it with FNR up to 96-100%. A trivial evasion (capping malicious documents per target question below 3) defeats the clique-detection mechanism by definition, since a clique requires at least three mutually connected nodes. Detection quality is sensitive to embedding-model and similarity-function choice (ablations show dot product and ANCE perform markedly worse than cosine similarity with Contriever-msmarco), meaning practical deployment requires careful, dataset-specific tuning. Step I (kNN graph construction over the full corpus) is reported as the dominant runtime cost, and CleanBase-w/o-Step-I is computationally infeasible on NQ (2.34 billion edges) even as an ablation baseline, indicating scaling to very large or fast-growing knowledge databases is nontrivial. Utility impact is measured mainly via aggregate Hit/Recall/ACC on sampled non-target questions and via a supplementary generated-question set targeting exactly the falsely-flagged documents; the authors themselves note utility loss depends on benign-content redundancy in the corpus, so results may not generalize to knowledge databases with sparser, less redundant content.

quantitative_results: Average FPR across 6 datasets and 9 attacks: CleanBase 1.9%, PromptGuard 27.9%, DataSentinel 0.4%
Average FNR range across attacks: CleanBase 0.0%-34.2%, PromptGuard 0.0%-44.8%, DataSentinel 19.0%-99.2%
HotpotQA under PoisonedRAG: ASR drops from 97% to 6% with CleanBase deployed
Average end-to-end ASR across all 9 attacks and 6 datasets: CleanBase 9%, TrustRAG ~9%, PromptGuard ~10%, MetaSecAlign 38.5%, DataSentinel 42.6% (worst)
CleanBase average FNR on PoisonedRAG-W (PW): 34.2% vs. DataSentinel 97.2% and PromptGuard 44.8%
CleanBase average FNR on Phantom (PT): 16.5% vs. DataSentinel 99.2% and PromptGuard 29.4%
Ablation: removing Step II (pruning) pushes CleanBase FPR above 99%

Adaptive white-box attack (minimizing malicious-document similarity): CleanBase average FNR rises to 96.1%, post-defense ASR rises to 37.3% (vs. 20.0% ASR / 20.3% FNR for the black-box adaptive variant)
Runtime: Vanilla retriever 8.4s/question, TrustRAG ~2x (19.9s), ReliabilityRAG ~20x (162.9s) on an RTX 4090
Retriever/embedding-model ablation on FiQA+PoisonedRAG-B: Cosine Similarity FPR 0.8%/FNR 4.4% vs. Dot Product FPR 1.8%/FNR 86.2% vs. Euclidean Distance FPR 1.0%/FNR 2.0%
CleanBase + MetaSecAlign combination on FEVER/PW: ASR decreases by 10 percentage points vs. CleanBase alone; Table 12 reports a 17% ACC increase for the combination on generated utility questions
baselines_compared: ReliabilityRAG (secures retriever, evaluated only on NQ/NA due to inefficiency)
